source_file: The corporate social credit system in China and its transnational impact.txt
text:
companies to adopt the oﬃcial position of the CCP on foreign policy
matters.70 While such comments can be genuine and could be considered
a matter of PR, it has been found that 448 millions of social media comments
a year are not genuine opinions but fabrications by government employees
linked to the propaganda oﬃce.71 Thus, some data that ﬂow into the score
might not be focussed on economic trustworthiness and quality standards
but political loyalty. An algorithm might carry out the ultimate assessment72
but what happens ‘backstage’, meaning the operationalisation of the system
(how is data gathered, aggregated and analysed)73, sometimes remains
opaque. In local models in Qingzhen and Rongcheng transparency is
lacking as to how exactly scores are assigned to individuals and organis-
ations,74 yet, Rongcheng is praised to have the most developed and compre-
hensive social credit system that serves as a role model for other cities and
regions.75 In this way, discourse can be controlled and this discourse,
enriched with claims to knowledge and right, enters into the sanction-
beneﬁt system and feeds into a knowledge structure that serves a political
project.
   The CSCS does not draw a clear line between legal and illegal behaviour
but uses ‘scale ratings’,76 a feature that provides a ‘continuous scale between
“good” and “bad” behaviour’. This nudges the actors towards compliance77
and creates competition between companies to come as close to ‘good behav-
iour’ as possible and provides an opportunity to pressure companies in a
more indirect way than when there is merely the legal-illegal distinction. A
ﬁne for illegal behaviour is more easily paid than a loss of reputation is
repaired. A loss of reputation does not need to be linked to illegal behaviour,
yet it can cause business partners and customers to change their behaviour
